no acute intracranial abnormality.base_explanation: This means that no urgent problems such as bleeding, stroke, or major injury were seen in the brain at the time of the scan.
no acute intracranial abnormality.follow_up_note: This is generally a reassuring finding.

mild cerebral atrophy.base_explanation: This term means there is a small reduction in brain volume. It is commonly seen with aging and does not automatically indicate a serious condition.
mild cerebral atrophy.follow_up_note: A doctor may consider symptoms and medical history to decide if further evaluation is needed.

moderate cerebral atrophy.base_explanation: This finding indicates a more noticeable reduction in brain volume than mild atrophy. It can be associated with aging or chronic neurological conditions.
moderate cerebral atrophy.follow_up_note: Clinical correlation helps determine whether this finding is related to symptoms.

white matter changes.base_explanation: White matter changes refer to small areas in the brain where long-term blood flow changes may have occurred. These findings are common, especially with increasing age.
white matter changes.follow_up_note: Doctors often interpret this together with factors such as blood pressure and diabetes.

chronic small vessel ischemic changes.base_explanation: This term describes long-standing changes in small blood vessels of the brain, often related to aging or vascular risk factors.
chronic small vessel ischemic changes.follow_up_note: Management usually focuses on controlling risk factors rather than treating the scan finding itself.

old infarct.base_explanation: An old infarct indicates an area of the brain that was affected by reduced blood flow in the past. This does not represent a recent event.
old infarct.follow_up_note: Doctors consider history and symptoms to determine its relevance.

acute infarct.base_explanation: An acute infarct refers to a recent area of reduced blood flow in the brain that requires prompt medical attention.
acute infarct.follow_up_note: Immediate evaluation by healthcare professionals is important.

intracranial hemorrhage.base_explanation: This term means there is bleeding within the skull. The significance depends on the location and amount of bleeding.
intracranial hemorrhage.follow_up_note: This finding usually requires urgent medical evaluation.

hydrocephalus.base_explanation: Hydrocephalus refers to an increased amount of fluid within the brain spaces, which can place pressure on brain tissue.
hydrocephalus.follow_up_note: Further evaluation helps determine the cause and clinical significance.

mass lesion.base_explanation: A mass lesion refers to an abnormal area seen in the brain that occupies space. The exact nature of this finding depends on further evaluation.
mass lesion.follow_up_note: Additional tests are often needed to understand the cause.
